section.title: Israel's Renewed Apostasy
section.sectionRationale: The calm of Tola and Jair is shattered. Israel plunges deeper than before, serving a whole catalogue of foreign gods, and the LORD sells them into the hands of the Philistines and Ammonites. When they cry out, God does something startling: He recounts His past deliverances and then refuses, telling them to cry to the gods they have chosen. Only when they put away their idols and throw themselves on His mercy does the text say He could no longer bear their misery. This movement lays bare both the depth of Israel's sin and the astonishing tenderness of God, and it sets the stage for Jephthah's rise.
section.thesis: Israel's apostasy sinks to a new depth, and God's own words of refusal expose how empty their repeated repentance has been; yet when they finally put away their false gods and cast themselves wholly on Him, the LORD, grieved to the heart by their misery, moves again toward His undeserving people.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the plunge into idolatry and its judgment, verses 6 to 9; the cry of confession, verse 10; God's stinging refusal, verses 11 to 14; and true repentance met by God's grief and compassion, with Ammon and Israel drawn up for war, verses 15 to 18.

verses.text: And again the Israelites did evil in the sight of the LORD. They {{abad|served}} the Baals, the Ashtoreths, the gods of Aram, Sidon, and Moab, and the gods of the Ammonites and Philistines. Thus they {{azab|forsook}} the LORD and did not serve Him.
So the anger of the LORD burned against Israel, and He sold them into the hands of the Philistines and Ammonites,
who that very year harassed and oppressed the Israelites, and they did so for eighteen years to all the Israelites on the other side of the Jordan in Gilead, the land of the Amorites.
The Ammonites also crossed the Jordan to fight against Judah, Benjamin, and the house of Ephraim, and Israel was in deep distress.
Then the Israelites {{zaaq|cried out}} to the LORD, saying, “We have sinned against You, for we have indeed forsaken our God and served the Baals.”

units.label: God's stinging refusal
units.note: go and cry out to the gods you have chosen

verses.text: The LORD replied, “When the Egyptians, Amorites, Ammonites, Philistines,
Sidonians, Amalekites, and Maonites oppressed you and you cried out to Me, did I not save you from their hands?
But you have forsaken Me and served other gods, so I will no longer save you.
Go and cry out to the gods you have chosen. Let them save you in your time of trouble.”

units.label: Repentance met by God's compassion
units.note: He could no longer bear the misery of Israel

verses.text: “We have sinned,” the Israelites said to the LORD. “Deal with us as You see fit; but please deliver us today!”
So they put away the foreign gods from among them and served the LORD, and He could no longer bear the {{amal|misery}} of Israel.
Then the Ammonites were called to arms and camped in Gilead, and the Israelites assembled and camped at Mizpah.
And the rulers of Gilead said to one another, “Whoever will launch the attack against the Ammonites will be the head of all who live in Gilead.”

abad.gloss: To serve, work for, worship. The same verb covers both slavery and worship, so to serve other gods is to become their slave. Israel served the Baals and a long list of foreign gods, binding themselves to the very powers that could not save them, and only after putting them away do they serve the LORD again.

azab.gloss: To forsake, abandon, leave. Twice the passage says Israel forsook the LORD, and God echoes it back: you have forsaken Me. It is covenant desertion, the deliberate turning of the heart away from the God who redeemed them, and it is the root of the whole crisis.

zaaq.gloss: To cry out, call for help in distress. Israel cries out to the LORD under oppression, the recurring reflex of the judges cycle. Here God pointedly turns the word back on them, telling them to cry out instead to the gods they chose, exposing how shallow their crying has often been.

amal.gloss: Trouble, misery, toil, suffering. When Israel finally puts away their idols, the LORD could no longer bear the amal, the misery, of Israel. The word paints their affliction, and the verse pictures God moved and grieved to the heart by the suffering of the people He loves despite their sin.

basic.context: After the quiet years of Tola and Jair, Israel falls again, and further than ever. Instead of a single idol, they serve a whole roster of foreign gods, the gods of every surrounding nation, and utterly forsake the LORD. So He hands them over to the Philistines and Ammonites, who crush them for eighteen years until Israel is in deep distress. They cry out and confess their sin, but this time God answers with a stinging refusal: He lists the many times He has already saved them, then says, in effect, go and cry to the gods you chose; let them save you. It is a shocking moment. Only when Israel actually puts away their idols and throws themselves on His mercy does the text say the LORD could no longer bear their misery. The stage is then set for the coming battle with Ammon and the rise of Jephthah.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative that also functions as a searching mirror. God's refusal is not a change in His character but a real response to hollow repentance; Israel had cried out before and then returned to their idols. The turning point is not the words we have sinned, which they had said before, but the deeds: they put away the foreign gods and served the LORD. We must not read God's grief as weakness or as being manipulated; rather the text lets us see, in human terms, the genuine anguish of a God who loves a people who keep wounding Him, and who moves toward them out of mercy, not obligation.
basic.originalLanguages: Israel abad, served, a long list of gods, the same verb used for slavery, so their worship was really bondage; and they azab, forsook, the LORD, covenant desertion. They zaaq, cried out, in distress, the recurring reflex of the cycle, and God turns the word back on them. At last, when they put the idols away, the LORD could no longer bear their amal, their misery, the word picturing a God grieved to the heart by the suffering of His wayward people.
basic.typology: Israel's endless cycle of sin, judgment, shallow crying, and relapse exposes the need for a deliverance that goes deeper than any judge could give, one that changes the heart. God's refusal to be used as a mere rescue service, followed by His grief and mercy toward the truly repentant, points forward to the gospel, where the wrath sin deserves and the mercy sinners need meet at the cross of Christ.
basic.christ: This passage shows a God who justly refuses to save those who use Him and abandon Him, yet who is moved to the heart by the misery of His people. In Christ, both come together: God does not simply overlook sin, He bears its full weight; and He is so moved by our misery that He gives His own Son. Where Israel put away idols and cast themselves on mercy, the gospel calls us to turn from every false god to the God who, in Jesus, could no longer bear to leave us in our misery.
basic.god: God is holy and just: His anger burns against covenant treachery, and He will not be treated as a convenient rescuer to be summoned and then abandoned. Yet He is also astonishingly tender. The same God who refuses hollow repentance is grieved to the heart by His people's suffering and moves toward them the moment their turning is real. We learn that God takes both sin and sinners seriously, judging the one while yearning over the other.
basic.crossReferences: Judges 2 sets out the cycle of apostasy this passage repeats. Deuteronomy 32 shows the LORD provoked to anger by other gods yet compassionate toward His people. Hosea 11 pictures God's heart recoiling within Him, unwilling to give up His people. And Luke 15 shows the Father running to the returning prodigal, the same mercy toward the repentant.
basic.oneStory: Israel sinks to a new low, and God's refusal exposes how empty their repeated repentance has been. Yet when they finally put away their idols and cast themselves on His mercy, the LORD is grieved by their misery and moves again to save. It is the judges cycle laid bare, and it deepens the ache for a Deliverer who can break the cycle by changing the heart, the ache the whole book carries toward Christ.

context.literary: This is the extended prologue to the Jephthah account, and it follows the familiar cycle of Judges: sin, servitude, supplication, and salvation. But the author slows the pattern down and adds something new, an extended dialogue in which God actually refuses. The long list of foreign gods in verse 6 and the long list of past deliverances in verses 11-12 are set deliberately side by side, measuring the depth of Israel's ingratitude. The unit ends with the armies gathering and the leaderless question of who will fight Ammon, which propels the story into chapter 11.
context.historical: The Ammonites lived east of the Jordan and pressed hard on the Israelite tribes of Gilead, while the Philistines pressured from the west, setting up the two great threats of this era, Ammon for Jephthah and Philistia later for Samson. The catalogue of gods, the Baals and Ashtoreths of Canaan and the deities of Aram, Sidon, Moab, Ammon, and Philistia, reflects the real religious pluralism of the region, where Israel was constantly tempted to hedge its worship among the gods of its neighbors.
context.canonical: The passage crystallizes the theology of the whole book, first stated in Judges 2:11-19: Israel serves other gods, the LORD gives them over to enemies, they cry out, and He raises a deliverer. Here the cycle is examined and strained, showing that the pattern cannot go on forever and that the people need more than repeated rescue. It anticipates the prophets' call to put away idols and return to the LORD, and ultimately the new covenant promise of a changed heart.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose the depth and repetitiveness of Israel's apostasy, to show through God's refusal that shallow, self-serving repentance is not true return, and to reveal both the justice and the deep compassion of the LORD, who nonetheless moves toward His people when their repentance becomes real.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes Israel's sin and God's response in a particular history; it does not prescribe idolatry, obviously, nor does God's stinging refusal become a formula that God always withholds help. What endures is the pattern of the human heart, the danger of using God, the call to genuine repentance that puts away idols, and the revelation of God's justice and mercy.

debates.question: Does God's refusal in verses 13-14 mean He truly would not save them?

views.label: A real but provisional refusal
views.case: God genuinely refuses to keep functioning as a rescue service for a people who cry out and then return to their idols; the refusal is a real rebuke meant to break their pattern, not an idle threat, and it does its work by driving them to actual repentance.

views.label: A pastoral shock to produce true repentance
views.case: God's words are calculated to expose the hollowness of their crying and to press them past mere words to deeds; the very refusal is an act of severe mercy aimed at bringing them to put away their gods, which is exactly what then happens.

debates.bearing: Either way, the passage warns against treating God as a convenient rescuer and shows that His severity here serves His purpose of leading His people to genuine, idol-forsaking repentance.

debates.question: How should we understand the statement that God could no longer bear Israel's misery?

views.label: Genuine divine compassion
views.case: The text lets us see, in human terms we can grasp, that God is truly moved by the suffering of His people; His mercy is not cold calculation but heartfelt grief over their misery, consistent with His self-revelation as compassionate and gracious.

views.label: Anthropopathic language for God's steadfast love
views.case: Scripture often describes God with human emotions to communicate truth about His unchanging character; the phrase conveys the reliability of His covenant love and mercy toward the repentant without implying He is overpowered by feeling.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm that God is genuinely merciful toward His repentant people, and neither reduces His mercy to mere sentiment or His justice to indifference.

study.originalLanguages: The words trace the whole tragedy. Israel abad, served, a catalogue of foreign gods, and because the same verb covers both slavery and worship, their worship was really bondage to powers that could not save. They azab, forsook, the LORD, deliberate covenant desertion, and God pointedly echoes the word back: you have forsaken Me. Under oppression they zaaq, cried out, the recurring reflex of the judges cycle, but God turns the very word against them, telling them to cry out instead to the gods they chose, so exposing how shallow their crying had been. The decisive change is not in their words but in their deeds: they put away the foreign gods and served, abad, the LORD. And then the moving climax: the LORD could no longer bear the amal, the misery, of Israel, a word for trouble and suffering that pictures God grieved to the heart over the people who keep wounding Him. The vocabulary preaches both the justice of a forsaken God and the tenderness of a God who will not finally abandon His own.

typology.type: The unbreakable cycle of sin, crying out, and relapse
typology.fulfillment: The need for a deliverance that changes the heart, given in the new covenant through Christ, Jeremiah 31:33 and Hebrews 8:10.

typology.type: God refusing to save the unrepentant yet grieved by their misery
typology.fulfillment: The cross, where God neither ignores sin nor abandons sinners, but bears the judgment and shows the mercy, Romans 3:25-26.

typology.type: Putting away foreign gods and casting themselves on mercy
typology.fulfillment: The gospel call to turn from idols to serve the living God through Jesus, 1 Thessalonians 1:9-10.

study.christ: Here is a God who justly refuses to be used, who lists His past mercies and says I will no longer save you to a people who forsake Him again and again, yet who cannot finally bear their misery. The gospel is where these two truths meet without compromise. At the cross God does not wink at Israel's sin or ours; He bears its full weight in His own Son, so that His justice is upheld. And there God shows how deeply He is moved by our misery, giving Jesus to rescue people who had every reason to be left to the gods they chose. Where Israel put away their idols and threw themselves on mercy, Christ calls us to turn from every false god to the God who, unable to leave us in our misery, came to save us Himself.
study.god: The chapter holds together two things we are tempted to split apart. God is holy and just: His anger burns against a people who serve every god but Him, and He refuses to be a rescue service summoned and then abandoned. Yet the same God is grieved to the very heart by His people's suffering and moves toward them the instant their repentance becomes real. He takes sin seriously enough to judge it and sinners seriously enough to yearn over them. This is not a God of cheap mercy or cold justice, but the LORD who is compassionate and gracious, slow to anger, and yet by no means clearing the guilty, the God fully revealed in Christ.

crossReferences.note: The cycle of apostasy, oppression, crying out, and deliverance that this passage repeats and probes.
Israel forsaking the God who made them and provoking Him with foreign gods, the sin named here.
God's heart recoiling within Him, unwilling to give up His people, the tenderness seen in verse 16.
Turning from idols to serve the living and true God, the true repentance this passage models.

study.oneStory: After a season of rest, Israel sinks lower than ever, serving every god of the nations and forsaking the LORD. His stinging refusal exposes how empty their repeated crying has been, but when they finally put away their idols and cast themselves on His mercy, He is grieved by their misery and moves once more to save. It is the judges cycle laid bare, showing that repeated rescue can never cure a wayward heart. The whole scene deepens the longing for a Deliverer who breaks the cycle from within, the longing answered in Christ, who bears our judgment and gives us a new heart to serve the living God.

quiz.q: How is Israel's apostasy in this passage described?

options.text: They served a whole catalogue of foreign gods and forsook the LORD entirely.
options.note: Verse 6 lists the Baals, Ashtoreths, and the gods of many nations, a new depth of idolatry.

options.text: They worshiped the Baals as they had before.
options.note: True in part, but this time the sin was broader, a whole roster of foreign gods.

options.text: They merely neglected the yearly feasts.
options.note: The sin was outright idolatry and forsaking the LORD, not just neglected feasts.

options.text: They remained faithful but were oppressed unjustly.
options.note: The opposite: they did evil and forsook the LORD, which brought the oppression.

quiz.q: How did the LORD first respond when Israel cried out and confessed?

options.text: He recounted His past deliverances and refused, telling them to cry to the gods they had chosen.
options.note: Verses 11-14 record God's stinging refusal, exposing their hollow repentance.

options.text: He immediately raised up a deliverer to save them.
options.note: That is the usual pattern, but here God first refuses before mercy comes.

options.text: He remained completely silent and gave no answer.
options.note: God was not silent; He answered pointedly, listing past rescues and refusing.

options.text: He destroyed them for their sin without any word.
options.note: He did not destroy them; He rebuked them and later showed compassion.

quiz.q: What actually turned the situation toward mercy?

options.text: They put away their foreign gods and served the LORD, not merely saying words.
options.note: Verse 16 shows the decisive change was in their deeds, casting themselves on His mercy.

options.text: They said the words we have sinned once more.
options.note: They had said this before; the real turn was putting away the idols, not the words alone.

options.text: They won a battle that impressed the LORD.
options.note: No victory turned God; the battle with Ammon is still ahead at this point.

options.text: They offered many sacrifices to buy His favor.
options.note: The text points not to bought favor but to genuine repentance that forsook idols.

quiz.q: What does the passage reveal about God's heart toward Israel?

options.text: He is just and refuses to be used, yet He is grieved to the heart by their misery.
options.note: Verse 16 holds His justice and deep compassion together.

options.text: He is loving and would never let His people suffer at all.
options.note: He does allow real judgment; the truth is both His justice and His compassion.

options.text: He is indifferent to whether His people suffer.
options.note: Far from indifferent, He could no longer bear their misery.

options.text: He is only angry and shows no mercy.
options.note: His anger is real, but so is the mercy that moves toward His repentant people.

quiz.q: How does this movement point toward the gospel of Christ?

options.text: God neither ignores sin nor abandons sinners; at the cross His justice and His mercy toward the miserable meet.
options.note: The passage's tension is resolved in Christ, who bears judgment and shows mercy, Romans 3:25-26.

options.text: It teaches that God saves anyone who says sorry without any change.
options.note: The passage stresses real repentance that puts away idols, not mere words.

options.text: It shows that God's mercy makes His justice unimportant.
options.note: Both matter fully; the cross upholds justice and mercy together.

options.text: It proves God finally gave up on His people forever.
options.note: He did not give up; He was moved by their misery and moved to save.

questions: Israel served the gods of every nation around them. What are the modern idols you are tempted to hedge your worship among, alongside the LORD?
God exposed how hollow their repeated crying had been. Is there a place where you say sorry to God with your words but keep returning to the same sin?
The turning point was not saying we have sinned but actually putting away the idols. What would it look like to put away, not just confess, a false god in your life?
The LORD could no longer bear Israel's misery. How does it change your view of God to see Him grieved to the heart over the suffering of wayward people?
At the cross God's justice and mercy meet. How does knowing that God neither ignores your sin nor abandons you free you to come to Him honestly today?

translationNotes.note: The Baals and Ashtoreths are the male and female fertility deities of Canaan; the verse then lists the gods of Aram, Sidon, Moab, Ammon, and Philistia, stressing how total Israel's idolatry had become.
The BSB footnote notes that where the Hebrew reads Maonites, some Septuagint manuscripts read Midianites; either way the list recalls God's many past deliverances.
He could no longer bear the misery of Israel renders a phrase describing God as grieved or impatient over their suffering; the word for misery, amal, means trouble, toil, or affliction.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Judges 10:6-18. Prose narrative. Verse 12 concatenates the two string entries around the footnote marker. No em or en dashes present in the source or introduced; none anywhere in the file. Sermon status none: no verified expository sermon on this specific movement located this session.
